flashcards.front: What is a Label Switched Router (LSR)?
flashcards.back: An LSR is a router in an MPLS network that participates in label-based packet forwarding. It performs label operations (push, swap, pop) to forward packets based on labels rather than IP routing tables.

flashcards.front: What are the three basic label operations in MPLS?
flashcards.back: 1) PUSH: Add a new label at the top of the packet 2) SWAP: Replace the existing label with a new one 3) POP: Remove the outer label (can be penultimate hop popping)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Edge LSR and Core LSR?
flashcards.back: Edge LSR (LER) handles label imposition/removal at network boundaries, classifying packets into FECs. Core LSR (LSR) primarily performs label swapping in the network core, focusing on fast label-based forwarding.

flashcards.front: What is a Label Switched Path (LSP)?
flashcards.back: A unidirectional virtual path established through an MPLS network. It's the logical circuit created by a series of LSRs that forward packets based on labels, following a specific route.
A group of IP packets that are forwarded in the same manner through an MPLS network. Packets are assigned to FECs based on criteria like destination IP prefix, QoS requirements, or VPN membership.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between LIB and LFIB?
flashcards.back: LIB (Label Information Base) contains all label bindings known to the LSR. LFIB (Label Forwarding Information Base) is the actual forwarding table used for data plane operations, containing only active label mappings.
An optimization where the penultimate LSR removes the label before sending the packet to the edge LSR (LER), instead of keeping the label. This reduces processing at the egress router.
A protocol used in MPLS to distribute label bindings between LSRs. It establishes LSPs and maintains label mappings for efficient packet forwarding.

flashcards.front: What is the MPLS label stack structure?
flashcards.back: MPLS uses a stack of labels (shim header). Each label is 32 bits: 20-bit label value, 3-bit experimental (QoS), 1-bit bottom-of-stack flag, and 8-bit TTL.

flashcards.front: What are the two planes in LSR architecture?
flashcards.back: Control Plane: Handles label distribution, routing protocols, and FEC assignment. Data Plane: Performs actual packet forwarding using LFIB based on labels.
The process of pushing one or more labels onto a packet when it enters the MPLS domain. Performed by the Ingress LSR (LER).

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Bottom-of-Stack (BoS) bit?
flashcards.back: The BoS bit (1 bit) indicates whether this label is the last (bottom) label in the stack. A value of 1 means it's the last label; 0 means more labels follow.

flashcards.front: How does LSR achieve faster forwarding than traditional routers?
flashcards.back: LSR uses simple label lookup (exact match) instead of longest prefix matching in IP routing tables. This reduces computational complexity and enables hardware-based forwarding at wire speed.

flashcards.front: What is implicit null and explicit null in MPLS?
flashcards.back: Implicit null (label 3): Penultimate hop pops label, egress receives untagged packet. Explicit null (label 0): Label is preserved with 0 value, used for QoS preservation at egress.

flashcards.front: What is the role of RSVP-TE in MPLS networks?
flashcards.back: RSVP-TE (Resource Reservation Protocol-Traffic Engineering) extends RSVP for label distribution and traffic engineering in MPLS, allowing explicit route establishment and resource reservation.
